title: Sorting Algorithms in Java
summary: Merge sort, quick sort, heap sort, stability, complexity table, and Arrays.sort internals.

questions.prompt: Merge sort worst-case time complexity:
questions.explanation: Always splits into equal halves and merges — O(n log n) regardless of input.

questions.prompt: Quick sort worst-case time complexity:
questions.explanation: Occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g. sorted input with last-element pivot).

questions.prompt: Which of these sorting algorithms is stable?
questions.explanation: Stable = equal elements keep their original relative order. Merge sort never swaps equal elements past each other.

questions.prompt: Arrays.sort(int[]) in Java uses:
questions.explanation: Java uses Yaroslavskiy's dual-pivot Quicksort for primitive arrays — fast but NOT stable.

questions.prompt: Arrays.sort(String[]) in Java uses:
questions.explanation: Object arrays use TimSort — stable and adaptive, O(n) on nearly-sorted data.

questions.prompt: Merge sort space complexity:
questions.explanation: Requires an O(n) auxiliary array to hold merged results.

questions.prompt: Heap sort space complexity:
questions.explanation: Sorts in-place — only the O(log n) heapify call stack, considered O(1) extra.

questions.prompt: Bubble sort best case with an early-exit flag (already sorted):
questions.explanation: Zero swaps occur on the first pass — the flag exits immediately after O(n) comparisons.

questions.prompt: Quick sort with a random pivot achieves on average:
questions.explanation: Random pivot keeps partitions balanced in expectation.

questions.prompt: Counting sort time complexity:
questions.explanation: One pass to count (O(n)), one pass over range (O(k)), one pass to output (O(n)).

questions.prompt: Which algorithms guarantee O(n log n) worst-case?
questions.explanation: Quick sort degrades to O(n²) worst case. Merge and heap are always O(n log n).

questions.prompt: Stability is important when:
choices: The sort key is the only field
Multiple sort passes must preserve prior ordering of equal elements
questions.explanation: E.g. sort by department then by salary — second sort must be stable to keep department order.

questions.prompt: Lomuto partition scheme uses which element as pivot?
questions.explanation: Lomuto always picks a[r] (last element) as the pivot.

questions.prompt: After one full pass of bubble sort on [5, 3, 1, 4]:
questions.code: // one complete left-to-right pass comparing adjacent pairs

questions.prompt: TimSort is a hybrid of:
questions.explanation: Runs insertion sort on small natural runs then merges them — excellent for real-world nearly-sorted data.

questions.prompt: To sort objects by a field in Java:
choices: Cast field to int and subtract
questions.explanation: Arrays.sort(arr, Comparator.comparingInt(o -> o.field)) is idiomatic.

questions.prompt: 3-way partition (Dutch National Flag) variant of Quicksort is best when:
choices: There are many duplicate elements equal to the pivot
questions.explanation: Groups all pivot-equal elements in the middle, skipping them in recursive calls.

questions.prompt: Merge sort is preferred over quick sort when:
choices: Stability is required OR worst-case guarantee matters
Data fits entirely in CPU cache
questions.explanation: Merge provides stability and O(n log n) worst case at the cost of O(n) extra space.

questions.prompt: For sorting 8 elements inside TimSort, Java uses:
questions.explanation: Insertion sort has very low overhead for tiny arrays — TimSort uses it on small runs.

questions.prompt: Arrays.sort on int[] {3,1,2} — sort is:
questions.explanation: Arrays.sort sorts in ascending order by default.

flashcards.back: O(n log n) always. Stable. O(n) extra space. Divide → recurse → merge.
O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst. NOT stable. O(log n) stack space. Use random pivot.
O(n log n) always. NOT stable. O(1) extra. Build max-heap then extract.
Dual-pivot Quicksort: fast in practice, NOT stable.
TimSort: stable, adaptive, O(n) on nearly-sorted data.
Equal elements keep their original relative order after sorting.
O(n+k) for integers in [0, k). Non-comparison — beats O(n log n) lower bound.
Arrays.sort(arr, (a,b) -> a.field - b.field) — custom sort key in one line.

project.brief:
Write SortingLab.java with:
1. mergeSort(int[] a) — recursive, in-place via auxiliary array
2. quickSort(int[] a) — random pivot to avoid O(n²) on sorted input
3. topKFrequent(int[] nums, int k) — HashMap + min-heap of size k
4. A main() that times mergeSort vs Arrays.sort on a shuffled 100 000-element array.

checklist.text: mergeSort passes all correctness tests including duplicates
quickSort uses random pivot (Math.random() or swap with random index before partition)
topKFrequent returns exactly k most frequent elements
Timing output shows both complete in < 200 ms on 100k elements

hints: mergeSort: allocate the temp array once at the top level — pass it down to avoid repeated allocation.
quickSort: swap arr[l + random % (r-l+1)] with arr[r] before calling Lomuto partition.
topKFrequent: min-heap of size k — add every element, then poll when pq.size() > k.
